title: Outlive: The Science and Art of Longevity
subtitle: The Science and Art of Longevity
author: Peter Attia, MD
excerpt: A #1 New York Times bestseller that redefines the public conversation around aging. Attia presents the Medicine 3.0 framework—a proactive, prevention-first approach to extending both lifespan and healthspan through exercise, nutritional biochemistry, sleep optimization, and emotional health.
rationale: Root book #1 for longevity-research-and-aging. Outlive is the defining longevity book of the 2020s, introducing the Medicine 3.0 framework and synthesizing exercise science, nutritional biochemistry, sleep research, and emotional health into a unified longevity protocol.
keyIdeas: Medicine 3.0 shifts from reactive treatment to proactive prevention of chronic disease
The Four Horsemen—heart disease, cancer, neurodegeneration, metabolic dysfunction—share common roots in inflammation and insulin resistance
Exercise, particularly improving VO2 max, is the single most powerful longevity intervention
Healthspan—the period of life free from disease—is more important than lifespan alone
Emotional health and relationships are foundational to true longevity
keyTakeaways: Understanding the limitations of reactive medicine and the case for proactive prevention
Building an exercise program around Zone 2 training, VO2 max intervals, strength, and stability
Reframing nutrition as biochemistry rather than dietary dogma
Prioritizing sleep for glymphatic clearance and metabolic health
Recognizing emotional health as a prerequisite for a life worth extending
whoShouldRead: Anyone interested in the science of longevity and health optimization
People with a family history of heart disease, cancer, or Alzheimer's
Fitness enthusiasts seeking evidence-based training protocols
Readers who want to understand the biology of aging
whoShouldSkip: Those looking for quick fixes or simple prescriptions
Readers averse to detailed biochemical explanations
People who cannot afford or access advanced biomarker testing
Readers resistant to the idea of significant lifestyle change
